chemicals.concentration_notes: ◦ Concentration: The toxin is found in the entire plant, but it is specifically concentrated in the fruit (the scarlet berries), which may be attractive to pets. ◦ Potency: The plant is considered to have low toxicity. Veterinary sources note that a "large exposure dose" is typically required to cause toxicity. Small ingestions often result in no clinical signs, while severe cases (rare) may requi
chemicals.description: in cats: ◦ Gastrointestinal Irritation: The compound acts primarily as a mild irritant to the gastrointestinal tract. Ingestion generally leads to gastrointestinal discomfort rather than systemic organ failure. 4. Notes on
chemicals.name: Aucubin (classified as an acid-labile glycoside)

description: Japanese Aucuba (Aucuba japonica), also called Japanese Laurel or Spotted Laurel, is a common garden and houseplant shrub that is mildly toxic to cats. It contains aucubin, an iridoid glycoside found throughout the plant, along with irritant saponins. The bright scarlet berries may attract curious cats and represent the most accessible toxic source.
name: Japanese Aucuba
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: aucubin (iridoid glycoside) and irritant saponins; berries and leaves are the main exposure route
Mechanism: aucubin causes GI irritation; large doses may produce mild hepatotoxicity in cats
Symptoms: vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, lethargy; typically mild and self-limiting with small ingestions
Berries are visually appealing to cats — restrict access during berry season
Contact your vet if your cat ingests berries or shows more than mild GI symptoms

symptoms.name: Gastrointestinal Discomfort (Vomiting, Diarrhea)
symptoms.notes: ◦ The toxic compound, aucubin, is found throughout the plant but is concentrated in the fruit (scarlet berries). ◦ Treatment usually involves antiemetics (to stop vomiting) and fluid therapy for rehydration if the case is severe, but often no treatment is needed for small ingestions.

treatments.description: For known small ingestions, the veterinarian may recommend monitoring the cat at home or in the clinic without administering medication or decontamination procedures.
treatments.name: Clinical Monitoring / No Medical Intervention
treatments.notes: Veterinary literature states that for "small dose exposure cases," the recommended treatment is "None." Toxicity usually requires a large exposure, and the plant primarily causes mild gastrointestinal discomfort.Rehydration Therapy

treatments.description: The administration of fluids (typically subcutaneous or intravenous) to restore hydration levels in the cat.
treatments.name: Fluid Therapy / Rehydration
treatments.notes: This is necessary in "severely affected cases" where the cat has experienced significant fluid loss due to vomiting or diarrhea caused by the plant's glycoside, aucubin.Gastrointestinal Support

treatments.description: The veterinarian administers medication to suppress nausea and stop vomiting.
treatments.name: Administration of Antiemetics
treatments.notes: Used in severe cases to manage the primary symptom of gastrointestinal discomfort.Electrolyte Management

treatments.description: Supplements are administered to restore the balance of essential minerals lost through persistent vomiting or diarrhea.
treatments.name: Electrolyte Replacement
treatments.notes: This is listed as a necessary step for "severely affected cases" alongside rehydration.
